topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon. My stomach clenched, a familiar, tight knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in fifteen years—a smirk I remembered too well, etched into the digital image. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, trembling slightly. A wave of cold washed over me, chilling the warmth in my chest. Why now? After all this time, after I’d built a life that felt solid, this ghost from my past decided to reappear. I quickly minimized the message, shoving my phone face down on the desk. The silence of my room suddenly felt heavy, suffocating. I stared at the wall, tracing the pattern of the paint with my eyes, wishing the floor would just swallow me whole.

[story 2]

"Oh, come on," I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible. The little ping signaled a new connection request, and my shoulders immediately hunched inward, pulling my arms tight across my chest. I didn't even look at the name; I could feel the history clinging to the digital invitation like stale smoke. Every nerve ending seemed to prickle with a low-grade anxiety. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, but I couldn't bring myself to drink. The steam curled up, mocking the energy I desperately wished I possessed. *Just ignore it. It means nothing.* But the urge to check it, to see if they’d sent a message, was overwhelming. I set the mug down with a soft clink, the sound echoing loudly in the sudden emptiness of the apartment.

[story 3]

I felt a distinct sinking sensation, like stepping off a high ledge and realizing there was no net beneath me. The request popped up, and my breath hitched. It was him. The sheer shock wasn't loud; it was a deep, internal shudder that ran from my core to my fingertips. I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly at the edges. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. A strange, heavy lethargy settled over my limbs, making even simple movements feel monumental. I slowly reached for the phone, my hand moving with agonizing slowness, as if wading through thick syrup. I didn't press accept; instead, I let the notification sit there, a tiny, unwelcome stone lodged in my chest, and focused instead on the dust motes dancing in the single shaft of sunlight cutting across the floor.
